bibliographic_info.title: Precise Point Positioning Performance Evaluation of QZSS Centimeter Level Augmentation Service

product_source.claimed: QZSS Centimeter Level Augmentation Service (CLAS) L6 signal
grounding_quotes: The Japanese Quasi-Zenith Satellite System(QZSS) provides Centimeter-Level Augmentation Service(CLAS) through L6 signal.

Based on the measured data of AIRA, STK2, TSK2 and USUD of four MGEX stations in Japan, this paper analyzes the PPP augmentation performance of L6 augmentation signal by post static PPP, and compares it with the calculation results of QZSS final orbit and clock products.

domain_relevance.reasoning: The paper evaluates PPP performance using QZSS CLAS L6 augmentation, not BDS-3 PPP-B2b. It is related to GNSS augmentation services.
grounding_quotes: Precise Point Positioning Performance Evaluation of QZSS Centimeter Level Augmentation Service
The L6 augmentation messages ... are used for a PPP-RTK service.
this paper analyzes the PPP augmentation performance of L6 augmentation signal by post static PPP

ionospheric_handling.dcb_missing_warning: Code bias included in CLAS messages but no DCB handling source specified.
grounding_quotes: The L6 augmentation messages includes satellite orbit correction, clock correction, phase bias, code bias, troposphere correction, ionosphere correction and other augmentation messages.
The L6 augmentation messages includes satellite orbit correction, clock correction, phase bias, code bias, troposphere correction, ionosphere correction and other augmentation messages.

research_problem.problem_statement: Evaluate the PPP augmentation performance of QZSS CLAS L6 signal and compare it with QZSS final orbit and clock products.
research_questions: What is the accuracy of orbit and clock augmented by L6 signal compared to final products?
Can static PPP based on L6 signal achieve centimeter-decimeter level positioning?
Why does a gap exist between CLAS augmented products and final products?
research_problem.knowledge_gap: CLAS is designed for PPP-RTK service, and its PPP augmented service is out of scope of its pre-service, potentially limiting positioning accuracy for standard PPP.

technical_route.overall_flow: Collect MGEX station observation data, apply QZSS L6 augmentation messages for static PPP post-processing, and compare against QZSS final orbit and clock products.
key_steps: Acquire observation data from AIRA, STK2, TSK2, USUD MGEX stations
Obtain L6 augmentation messages from QZSS CLAS
Perform post-static PPP using L6 corrections
Compute positioning results and compare with QZSS final products
grounding_quotes: Based on the measured data of AIRA, STK2, TSK2 and USUD of four MGEX stations in Japan, this paper analyzes the PPP augmentation performance of L6 augmentation signal by post static PPP, and compares it with the calculation results of QZSS final orbit and clock products.

method.description: Post-static PPP using QZSS CLAS L6 augmentation messages, with benchmark provided by QZSS final orbit and clock products.
method.baseline_method: QZSS final orbit and clock products

experiments.description: Static PPP performance evaluation using QZSS CLAS L6 signal and comparison with QZSS final products.
experiments.setup: Four MGEX stations in Japan: AIRA, STK2, TSK2, USUD; post-static PPP processing.
experiments.conditions: L6 augmentation messages applied; comparison with QZSS final orbit and clock products.
grounding_quotes: Based on the measured data of AIRA, STK2, TSK2 and USUD of four MGEX stations in Japan, this paper analyzes the PPP augmentation performance of L6 augmentation signal by post static PPP, and compares it with the calculation results of QZSS final orbit and clock products.
Based on the measured data of AIRA, STK2, TSK2 and USUD of four MGEX stations in Japan
The static PPP based on L6 signal can realize centimeter-decimeter level positioning

main_results.summary: Static PPP using L6 signal achieves centimeter-decimeter level positioning but with lower accuracy than QZSS final products. The degradation is attributed to the fact that CLAS orbit/clock corrections are not real-time precise corrections for standard PPP (they are optimized for PPP-RTK).
key_findings: The accuracy of orbit and clock augmented by L6 signal is still lower than QZSS final products.
Static PPP using L6 signal can reach centimeter-decimeter level positioning.
The gap arises because CLAS PPP augmented service is out of scope of its pre-service; the published orbit/clock corrections are not real-time precise corrections.
limitations: CLAS corrections are not designed for standard PPP, leading to lower accuracy compared to final products.
Only static PPP mode was tested.
grounding_quotes: There is still a certain gap between the accuracy of orbit and clock augmented by L6 signal and the final products of QZSS.
The static PPP based on L6 signal can realize centimeter-decimeter level positioning, and the positioning accuracy is lower than that of the final products of QZSS.

Analysis reason: as CLAS are used to augment PPP-RTK services, PPP augmented service is out of scope of its pre-service. Therefore, the published orbit and clock correction are not a real-time precise orbit and clock correction,which leads to a gap between the corrected orbit and clock and the final products.

Centimeter-Level Augmentation Service (CLAS)

Users can receive augmented information and broadcast ephemeris in real-time, extract and correct augmented information. Corrected to the broadcast orbit to generate a more accurate real-time satellite orbit and clock offset to achieve high-precision PPP.

grounding_quotes: This paper mainly introduces the CLAS augmented service based on QZSS L6D signals

L6 signals are stored in SSR format using RTCM 10403.2 standard. L6 files are published hourly and can be downloaded from the official QZSS website (https://sys.qzss.go.jp/dod/en/archives/agree.html).

correction_types.orbit_correction: Correction in radial, along-track and cross-track directions relative to satellite APC; recovered via coordinate transformation from broadcast ephemeris.
correction_types.clock_correction: Clock difference correction using parameters C0, C1, C2. C1 and C2 are zero in QZSS. Corrected clock = broadcast clock - C0/c.

technical_route.overall_flow: Based on the excerpt, the flow includes: obtain broadcast ephemeris; receive CLAS orbit and clock corrections; transform orbit corrections from orbital coordinate system to ECEF; apply corrections to compute precise orbit and clock offset.
key_steps: Receive CLAS correction messages (type 11, type 2, type 3)
Determine applicable correction based on network ID
Recover precise orbit by transforming radial/along/cross corrections to ECEF and subtracting from broadcast position
Recover precise clock offset by applying C0 correction (C1, C2 = 0) to broadcast clock
grounding_quotes: CLAS augmentation algorithm stipulates: if the Network ID of type 11 correction information in Table 1 is the same as the Network ID of the user's location, then the correction information of orbit and clock in type 11 correction information shall be adopted for correction.

method.description: Standard method to convert QZSS CLAS orbit corrections from satellite body-fixed system to ECEF and apply to broadcast ephemeris; clock correction using simplified single parameter model.
method.algorithm_details: Uses cross product of satellite position and velocity vectors to define radial (r), along-track (a), cross-track (c) unit vectors; transforms corrections to ECEF and subtracts from broadcast orbit. Clock correction applies C0 term only.

research_problem.problem_statement: Evaluate the accuracy of real-time orbit and clock error from QZSS L6 augmentation.
research_questions: What is the accuracy of real-time orbit and clock corrections from QZSS L6 augmentation?

technical_route.overall_flow: Unify space-time datum (GPST, ITRF2008), apply antenna phase center correction using IGS14 and dual-frequency ionospheric combination, compare augmented orbit (R, A, C) and clock with QZSS precise products, compute RMS and SISURE.
key_steps: Unify time and coordinate systems to GPST and ITRF2008
Antenna phase center correction using IGS14 antenna file and dual-frequency ionospheric combination
Compute orbit errors in radial, along-track, cross-track directions and their RMS
Compute satellite clock errors using a secondary difference method and RMS
Calculate SISURE from orbit and clock errors using Equation (2.4)
grounding_quotes: The space-time datum is unified, and the time system is unified to GPST, and the coordinate datum is unified to ITRF2008 coordinate system.
the IGS14 antenna file was used to correct the orbit coordinates from the antenna phase center to the satellite mass by using the dual-frequency ionospheric combination.
Then compare the orbit R, A, and C to get the errors of the augmented orbit and QZSS precise orbit in the R, A, and C direction, and then calculate the RMS values in each direction.
Finally, the RMS value of the satellite clock offset error is calculated.
after the accuracy analysis of the orbit and clock offset, the following formula was used to calculate the SISURE of different satellites

method.method_name: Orbit and clock error assessment using QZSS precise ephemeris as reference
method.description: Comparison of real-time L6 augmented GPS orbit/clock against QZSS final precise orbit (15 min) and clock (5 min), with RMS in R/A/C directions, secondary difference clock error, and SISURE calculation.
method.algorithm_details: Unify datum, antenna phase center correction, orbit/clock differencing, RMS and SISURE.
method.baseline_method: QZSS precise orbit (15 min interval) and clock (5 min interval) as reference

formulas.context_quote: In Equation (2.4), ω_R and ω_A,C are the contribution factors of radial direction R and along-track direction A and cross-track direction C, c are the speed of light, dt are the error of satellite clock, and ΔR, ΔA, ΔC are the errors in the radial direction, along-track direction and cross-track direction of orbit, respectively.

experiments.description: Accuracy analysis of QZSS L6 augmented real-time orbit and clock error for GPS satellites
experiments.setup: Real-time L6 orbit/clock vs. QZSS precise orbit (15 min) and clock (5 min) as reference; coordinate system ITRF2008, time GPST, IGS14 antenna file for phase center correction.
grounding_quotes: Relevant data of DOY 258-260 days in 2019 were selected for experiments to analyze the augmented orbit and clock difference accuracy.

GPS precise orbits with an epoch interval of 15 min and precise clock offset with an epoch interval of 5 min provided by QZSS were selected as references (https://sys.qzss.go.jp/dod/en/archives/pnt.html).

research_problem.problem_statement: Evaluate the PPP augmentation performance of QZSS L6D augmentation information.

technical_route.overall_flow: Use MGEX observation data and QZSS L6 products for post-static dual-frequency ionosphere-free PPP; compare with the same processing using QZSS final precise orbit and clock products; validate coordinates against IGS SINEX truth.
key_steps: Collect MGEX observation data from four Japanese stations
Apply L6D CLAS corrections (orbit, clock, code bias, phase bias, troposphere) within PPP
Perform parallel processing with QZSS final products as reference
Compute coordinate deviations in E, N, U against IGS SINEX
grounding_quotes: this paper selected the MGEX (Multi-GNSS Experiment) observation data of the four stations in Japan: AIRA, STK2, TSK2, and USUD. L6 products published by QZSS were used to perform a post static PPP processing of DOY 258-260 on the four stations. At the same time, the precise orbit and precise clock products published by QZSS were used for the same processing of the above data, and the processing results were taken as reference. Finally, the coordinates in the SINEX file of IGS on that day were used as the real value to calculate deviation statistics of PPP results in the directions of E, N and U.

method.method_name: Post-static dual-frequency ionosphere-free PPP with QZSS CLAS L6D corrections

method.baseline_method: QZSS final precise orbit and clock products

experiments.description: PPP evaluation of QZSS CLAS L6D corrections over DOY 258-260 on four Japanese stations

experiments.conditions: Static PPP, 15° elevation cut-off, troposphere: CLAS correction (CLAS) vs Saastamoinen model (QZSS), ambiguity not fixed, tidal and antenna phase centre corrections applied via models

metrics.convergence_time: Convergence to 20 cm (E, N) and 40 cm (U) at about 420 epochs (30s each?)
grounding_quotes: The positioning direction of ENU in CLAS plan converges to 20cm, 20cm and 40cm at about 420 (30s) of the period
horizontal accuracy is about 13cm

main_results.summary: CLAS augmented PPP achieves centimeter-decimeter level horizontal accuracy of about 13 cm, but convergence is slower and accuracy is worse compared to PPP using QZSS final precise ephemeris and clock products.
key_findings: CLAS augmented positioning error in E direction is at cm level, but fluctuates greatly in N direction (centimeter-decimeter).
Horizontal accuracy of four stations fluctuates between centimeters and decimeters.
Convergence rate using QZSS final precise ephemeris and clock product is significantly faster than that of CLAS augmented positioning.
PPP positioning results of final precise products are better than CLAS augmented results.
CLAS plan converges to 20cm (E,N) and 40cm (U) at about 420 epochs (30s).
RMS statistics are shown in Table 4 (not provided in this chunk).
limitations: Only four stations over three days
CLAS augmentation has lower accuracy and slower convergence than final products
grounding_quotes: the positioning error after the augmentation is smaller in the direction of E, at the level of cm; However, it fluctuates greatly in the direction of N and in the level of centimeter-decimeter.
The horizontal accuracy of the four stations fluctuates between centimeters and decimeters.
the convergence rate of positioning using QZSS final precise ephemeris and clock product is significantly faster than that of CLAS augmented positioning.
The positioning results of the final precise ephemeris and clock products using QZSS are better than the augmented results.
The PPP positioning accuracy after CLAS augmentation can achieve centimeter-decimeter level positioning, horizontal accuracy is about 13cm

correction_types.orbit_correction: mentioned: radial precision 0.26m, along-track 1.05m, cross-track 0.94m, 3D 1.44m after augmentation
correction_types.clock_correction: mentioned: clock offset precision 0.46m after augmentation
grounding_quotes: After the augmentation, the clock offset precision is about 0.46m, the radial average precision is about 0.26m, the along-track precision is about 1.05m, the cross-track precision is about 0.94m, the 3D precision is about 1.44m, and the SISURE precision is about 0.62m.

research_problem.problem_statement: Evaluate the performance of QZSS L6D augmentation information for static PPP positioning and compare with QZSS final precise products.
research_questions: What is the accuracy of static PPP using QZSS L6D augmentation?
How does it compare to using QZSS final orbits and clocks?
research_problem.knowledge_gap: L6D is oriented to PPP-RTK, but its PPP performance needs to be assessed.

technical_route.overall_flow: Acquire QZSS L6D augmentation information, apply to static PPP processing for four MGEX stations, compare with results using QZSS final precise orbit and clock.
key_steps: Collect L6D augmentation information from QZSS
Perform static PPP using L6D corrections
Compute positioning errors against known coordinates
Repeat PPP using QZSS final precise orbit and clock products as reference
grounding_quotes: In this paper, the performance of QZSS L6D augmentation information is evaluated, and uses the QZSS final orbit and clock products as references to evaluate the augmentation effect of L6D signals.

method.method_name: Standard PPP with QZSS CLAS L6D corrections
method.description: Static PPP using QZSS L6D augmentation information as corrections.
method.baseline_method: PPP using QZSS final precise ephemeris and clock products (QZF plan)

experiments.description: Static PPP positioning experiment on four MGEX stations (AIRA, TSK2, STK2, USUD) using QZSS L6D augmentation (CLAS plan) and QZSS final precise products (QZF plan).
experiments.setup: Uses observation data from MGEX, broadcast ephemeris from IGS, L6D and precise products from QZSS.
grounding_quotes: Table 4. RMS statistics of PPP positioning errors for two plans of four stations
Thanks to the precise products and L6 products provided by QZSS officials, the broadcast ephemeris data provided by IGS, and the station observation data provided by MGEX.

datasets.access_info: MGEX station observation data, IGS broadcast ephemeris, QZSS L6 products and precise products provided by QZSS officials.
grounding_quotes: Thanks to the precise products and L6 products provided by QZSS officials, the broadcast ephemeris data provided by IGS, and the station observation data provided by MGEX.
Table 4. RMS statistics of PPP positioning errors for two plans of four stations
The static PPP positioning horizontal accuracy based on QZSS L6D augmentation information is about 13cm and the vertical accuracy is about 16cm

After the augmentation, the clock offset precision is about 0.46m, the radial average precision is about 0.26m, the along-track precision is about 1.05m, the cross-track precision is about 0.94m, the 3D precision is about 1.44m, and the SISURE precision is about 0.62m.

main_results.summary: QZSS L6D augmentation provides orbit and clock corrections improving SISURE to 0.62m; static PPP horizontal accuracy 13cm, vertical 16cm, but still significantly worse than results with final precise products.
key_findings: Still large error compared to QZSS final precise products
Need further research on PPP-RTK performance of L6D and PPP performance of L6E
limitations: PPP results with L6D still have large errors compared to final products
grounding_quotes: The static PPP positioning horizontal accuracy based on QZSS L6D augmentation information is about 13cm and the vertical accuracy is about 16cm, which still has a large error with the final precise ephemeris and precise clock PPP positioning results of QZSS.
Further research is needed on the PPP-RTK performance of QZSS L6D augmentation information and the PPP performance of L6E augmentation information.
In this paper, the performance of QZSS L6D augmentation information is evaluated
